items.source: All Star Charts / Alfonso De Pablos
items.title: DXY closed week at 52-week highs — now testing the critical 100 level
items.idea: The U.S. Dollar Index $DXY closed Friday at a new 52-week high and is testing 100 — a level that Alfonso calls the single most important chart going into next week. A clean break and sustained hold above 100 would likely introduce turbulence for risk assets globally, as strong dollar = capital rotating away from risk. A failed breakout, conversely, reinforces the equity tailwind that has driven markets for the past year.
items.angle: DXY at 100 is the macro binary of the week. The setup is clean enough that both chartists and macro traders are watching it — which means it's crowded in terms of attention but the outcome is genuinely binary. Worth positioning around.
items.tweetA: The one chart everyone is staring at into next week: $DXY at 100. Break and hold above = turbulence for risk. Fail = equity tailwind intact. That's the entire macro setup in one line.
items.tweetB: DXY at new 52-week highs, testing 100. The dollar doesn't usually rip this quietly. If this breaks, the equity crowd is holding the wrong risk. If it fails, the range is confirmed and everyone who shorted looks wrong. This is NOT a boring level.

charts.desc: DXY weekly chart showing base formation and test of 100 level

items.title: The Age of Reverse De-Equitization: AI capex is ending the 15-year buyback engine that powered US equities
items.idea: Nearly 40% of all dollar value created in the Nasdaq 100 since 2013 is directly attributable to Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Meta — driven largely by $2T+ in buybacks between 2013 and 2025. Those same companies are now issuing equity to fund AI capex (Google, Meta, Oracle, Amazon all announcing fundraises). Net equity issuance turned net positive for the first time since 2018. The thesis: AI is reversing the structural tailwind that drove the entire mega-cap era — simultaneously consuming buyback capital, threatening 401k flows via white-collar job displacement, and adding new IPO supply.
items.angle: This reframes the AI bull case as the mechanism of its own undoing. The money that powered US equity valuations for 15 years (buybacks) is now being rerouted to AI capex, which funds equity issuance. First time since 2018 that net equity supply is expanding. That's a structural headwind the market hasn't priced.
items.tweetA: $2 trillion in buybacks from 2013–2025 powered 40% of Nasdaq gains. Now that money is going to AI capex instead — and the hyper-scalers are issuing equity to bridge the gap. Net equity supply turned positive for first time since 2018. The machine that drove the bull market is running in reverse.
items.tweetB: Google raises equity. Meta raises equity. Oracle raises equity. For 15 years, tech giants shrank their float. Now they're expanding it — to fund the very AI that may eliminate the white-collar jobs that funded 401k flows. The de-equitization trade has reversed, and nobody is calling it.

charts.desc: Hyper-scaler AI capex commitment table (massive off-balance sheet numbers); capex as % of EV trend shift chart — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: $SPCX micro-float quadruples in under one quarter — then doubles again by November
items.idea: SpaceX has a micro free float currently making $SPCX a plaything for gamma traders, but less than one quarter away the float quadruples. It then doubles again by early November. At SpaceX's current cash burn rate, a primary equity offering may not be far behind. The Blind Squirrel calls Google's equity raise a potentially more important turning point than the SpaceX IPO headline itself — because it signals the era of reverse de-equitization has begun.
items.angle: This is a specific, time-bound, near-term positioning setup. If you're long $SPCX as a momentum/narrative trade, the float unlock schedule creates a ceiling that's arriving faster than the crowd assumes. The gamma squeeze dynamic unwinds when float quadruples.
items.tweetA: $SPCX micro-float is everyone's favorite meme right now. What's missing from the conversation: the float quadruples in less than one quarter. Then doubles again by November. The gamma squeeze dynamic has a very specific expiration date.
items.tweetB: Buried in the Blind Squirrel note: SpaceX float unlock schedule shows a stock that's about to triple its available shares in two steps before year-end. Meanwhile, current cash burn may force a primary offering on top. The meme trade has a clock.

charts.desc: SpaceX shareholder unlock schedule showing float quadrupling then doubling

items.title: Infinite transformers wouldn't fix the grid — converters, inverters, and MMCs are the real bottleneck
items.idea: DOE economist Dana Golden runs a thought experiment: a genie provides unlimited transformers + gas turbines + streamlined permitting. Grid still can't keep up. The HVDC supply chain (Modular Multilevel Converters, DC filters, solid-state breakers) has essentially zero domestic US manufacturing capacity. Only 4 companies globally build HVDC converter stations: Hitachi Energy, Siemens Energy, GE Vernova, and Mitsubishi Electric. SiC crystal growth runs 0.1–0.5mm/hour at 2,200°C with 1–2 week cycle times. 'This is not a process you can rapidly scale.'
items.angle: The transformer meme is where awareness starts, but the actual AI power buildout is blocked by components 3-4 levels deeper in the supply chain that nobody covers. This is the kind of infrastructure bottleneck that doesn't announce itself — it just makes the capex projections impossible to fulfill on schedule.
items.tweetA: Give the US infinite transformers. Also gas turbines. Also perfect permitting. The grid still can't support the AI buildout. The actual bottleneck is HVDC converters, MMC modules, solid-state breakers — and there are basically 4 companies on earth that build them. SiC crystals grow at 0.5mm/hour. You can't rush this.
items.tweetB: The power grid conversation is stuck on transformers. That's like saying the chip shortage is about packaging. The real constraint is modular multilevel converters, wide-bandgap semiconductors, HVDC components. None of this has domestic US manufacturing capacity. The AI supply chain and the grid supply chain are the same supply chain.

charts.desc: Supply chain flow diagram for SiC semiconductors; risk matrix for power electronics; DOE framework scoring grid — CHART WORTH SAVING (the flow diagram)

items.title: China already pulled the trigger on gallium — the actual critical mineral chokepoint scores 100/125 in DOE risk framework
items.idea: GaN semiconductors (essential for grid inverters, data center power, EV charging) require gallium. The US has negligible domestic gallium production and minimal allied production. China already implemented export restrictions on gallium. The DOE risk framework scores gallium supply disruption at 100/125: threat probability >35% annually (essentially ongoing), vulnerability rated maximum (weeks-to-months disruption), consequence rated 4/5. SiC substrate shortage scored 64/125. Taiwan Strait crisis scores 50. The highest-risk scenario is already happening.
items.angle: The critical minerals discussion always clusters around lithium and cobalt. The actual chokepoint for the AI power buildout is gallium, which has already been weaponized. This connects directly to the AI capex story — all that infrastructure spending assumes component availability that may not be there.
items.tweetA: Everyone talks about critical minerals. The actual bottleneck for the AI power buildout: gallium. Required for every GaN semiconductor (inverters, data center power). US has negligible domestic production. China already imposed export restrictions. DOE risk score: 100/125. That ceiling has already been activated.
items.tweetB: The chip industry spent 3 years building awareness of semiconductor chokepoints. Power electronics is 5 years behind that conversation. Gallium export restrictions from China: already in effect. Risk score: 100/125. The AI buildout depends on a supply chain China has already demonstrated willingness to cut.

charts.desc: Risk matrix: gallium at top-right (highest risk/importance); US mine lead times vs global peers (25+ year average) — CHART WORTH SAVING (mine lead times)

items.title: AI buildout and grid supply chains have converged — US offshore wind 2030 projections now below previous 2027 projections
items.idea: Datacenter HVDC uses the same underlying power electronics as grid-scale HVDC. Both consume SiC and GaN devices at volume. The virtuous cycle that strengthened China's power electronics ecosystem (massive domestic demand for offshore wind, solar, EVs) is running in reverse in the US — offshore wind 2030 projections are now below the 2027 projections from two years ago. Public backlash to grid failures 'could halt AI right in its tracks.' The US needs to treat power electronics with the same strategic seriousness as GPUs.
items.angle: The frame here is that power electronics is to AI what GPU supply was to crypto mining — the physical constraint nobody priced until it was obvious. The offshore wind collapse is directly reducing demand for the US power electronics ecosystem, making future recovery harder.
items.tweetA: US offshore wind projections for 2030 are now below what we expected to achieve by 2027. That's the collapse of the domestic demand signal that would have built the power electronics ecosystem. Without demand, no ecosystem. Without ecosystem, no grid. Without grid, no AI. The chain is long and it's breaking early.
items.tweetB: A grid failure in the AI era isn't just a utility problem — it's a political event. Public backlash to blackouts ended independent system operator independence in the 1990s. Same dynamic today means a major grid incident could halt AI right in its tracks. The power grid is the AI industry's political risk.

charts.desc: US offshore wind capacity projections: 2030 target now below 2027 forecast from 2 years ago

items.title: Rollercoaster week — semis won again, banks over-loved then collapsed, SOX back at critical 14k
items.idea: A volatility-heavy week; semis won as usual. Banks got over-loved by Thursday then closed terribly into the expiration/rebalancing. SOX is back under 14k — a failure to recover here 'will look like a failure' and MU is the key stock to watch. DXY: even non-chart people can see the base; Meisler's prediction: 'If it gets to 102 the love will be too much.' GLD rallied right to resistance, then filled the gap — still in downtrend. Insiders neutral for an exceptionally long stretch. Citi Panic/Euphoria slightly off the boil. XRT at the same price as September.
items.angle: Meisler's sentiment framing is the edge here: 'even non-chart folks can see the DXY base' is actually a contrarian warning — consensus visibility of a setup often precedes the fade. Combined with insiders still flat and Panic/Euphoria barely moving off peak, the market internals look tired even as price holds.
items.tweetA: Helene Meisler on $DXY: 'Even the non-chart folks can see that base. If it gets to 102 the love will be too much.' Translation: the breakout is crowded before it happens. $GLD filled the gap after rallying to resistance. Insiders still flat. Panic/Euphoria barely moved. Market internals don't match the narrative.
items.tweetB: $SOX back under 14k. Meisler says if it stays here it 'will look like a failure.' $MU is the tell this week. Banks over-loved into Thursday, then collapsed. The week's pattern: everything looked great until expiration. That's not strength — that's positioning.

items.title: Pod shops lost $5.5bn in a single March week — synchronized drawdown exposes the independence myth
items.idea: In early March: Millennium and Point72 each lost ~$1.5bn, Citadel ~$1bn (fixed income/macro books), Balyasny ~$1bn (mostly rates) — all inside the same volatility spike. Gross leverage across multi-strat funds has climbed from 4x a decade ago to 12x today (net leverage ~4.5x), and pod shops account for >30% of US equity volume. At that share, when they cut the same crowded factor exposure simultaneously, they SET the price — which is why a coordinated unwind turns a normal volatility spike into $5.5bn in synchronized losses. The pod model's 'independence' premise breaks exactly when it's most needed.
items.angle: 12x gross leverage + 30% equity volume market share = system that moves prices against itself when it cuts. This isn't a bug in the pod model; it's the logical endpoint. The narrative sold to allocators was uncorrelated returns. March proved that when factor crowding is high enough, correlation is 1.0.
items.tweetA: Pod shops lost $5.5bn in a single March week. Millennium, Point72, Citadel, Balyasny — all down simultaneously. The product sold to allocators is 'independent books, uncorrelated returns.' What actually happened: 330+ pods running similar factor risk hedged in similar ways sharing one pool of liquidity. At 12x gross leverage and 30% of US equity volume, they move prices against themselves.
items.tweetB: The multi-strat business model works perfectly until it doesn't. 2025: Point72 +17.5%, Balyasny +16.7%, great year. March 2026: $5.5bn gone in days. Not a strategy failure — the books were fine. A structural failure: at 12x leverage and 30% market volume share, when the biggest platforms cut the same exposure, the market goes where they push it. Independence is a story; liquidity is physics.

items.title: Allocator exodus from multi-strat: 8% YoY decline in institutional interest, only 15% plan to raise exposure
items.idea: A capital-introduction survey of ~2,500 institutional investors recorded an 8% YoY decline in interest in multi-strategy funds. Goldman's mid-year review found only 15% of allocators planning to raise their multi-strat exposure — down sharply from the peak in 2022 and 2023. A March drawdown is a recruiting story by summer: Millennium reallocates from weakest pods, creating visible hiring signals. March losses concentrated in rates and fixed income books suggest quant compensation in those strategies compresses through the rest of 2026.
items.angle: The money that flooded into multi-strat at the 2022-2023 peak is starting to exit. If allocators are pulling back while pod shops still carry 12x gross leverage, the unwind dynamic from March can repeat — and next time the exits will be smaller as AUM shrinks.
items.tweetA: Multi-strat allocator interest down 8% YoY. Only 15% of institutions planning to raise exposure, vs peak 2022-23. The capital that chased uncorrelated returns into pod shops is rotating out. When leverage is 12x and AUM shrinks, the exit is uglier than the entrance.
items.tweetB: A March drawdown at Millennium becomes a recruiting story by summer — pods get cut, quants change desks. But the macro signal is bigger: institutional allocators are stepping back from multi-strat for the first time since the strategy went mainstream. What replaces it? That's the open question.

items.title: Warsh Fed — most important meeting since QE2 launch in 2010; market's bear flattening completely missed it
items.idea: Ironside argues the sharp bear flattening of the Treasury curve after the Warsh Fed meeting 'completely missed the implications' — the market was focused on a hawkish dot plot that is 'living on borrowed time.' The real signal: Warsh's five task forces aim to reduce the Fed's footprint, shorten SOMA duration, eliminate forward guidance, and shift cost-of-capital determination back to markets. Ironside: 'The Fed is not going to hike. If disinflation is wrong, they tighten via balance sheet.' Conclusion: 2s are a buy, curve will bull steepen. Even with the Iran War energy spike, breakeven inflation remains below September pre-cut levels.
items.angle: The key trade call is 2s = buy, bull steepener. The market priced the meeting as hawkish (bear flattening) when the actual message was structurally dovish (less footprint, less forward guidance, balance sheet as tightening tool not rate hikes). If right, this is a significant rates mispricing.
items.tweetA: Ironside: the bond market read the Warsh Fed meeting completely wrong. Bear flattening = hawkish? No. The actual message was: no rate hikes, tighten via balance sheet if needed, shrink SOMA duration, eliminate forward guidance. Even with the Iran energy spike, breakevens are BELOW pre-cut September levels. 2s are a buy.
items.tweetB: Warsh's most quoted line: 'When financial markets are just reflecting back what we've said, we're being blind to the most important source of information.' He's saying the Fed became an echo chamber. The five task forces are unwinding 15 years of QE mission creep. The market called it hawkish. Ironside calls it the most consequential meeting since QE2.

charts.desc: Breakeven inflation curve vs September pre-cut baseline — below despite Iran energy shock

items.title: Strikes on Moscow, Lavrov threatens 'massive regular strikes on Ukraine' — Oreshnik IRBM back in play
items.idea: Strikes on Moscow and elsewhere inside Russia occurred in recent days. Foreign Minister Lavrov warned Russia will 'carry out massive strikes on Ukraine on a regular basis.' Frankopan flags the Oreshnik — an intermediate-range ballistic missile 'supposedly capable of evading modern air defense systems' — amid renewed Kremlin signals about deploying its newest strategic weapon. Email partially paywalled; full Russia piece forthcoming next week.
items.angle: The sequence matters: Ukrainian strikes penetrating Moscow → Lavrov making maximal public threats → Kremlin signaling Oreshnik deployment. This is an escalation ladder, not stable deterrence. Energy and defense sector implications are immediate.
items.tweetA: Strikes hit Moscow. Lavrov threatens 'massive regular strikes on Ukraine.' The Kremlin is floating deployment of the Oreshnik — an IRBM supposedly capable of evading modern air defense. This is not a stable deterrence posture. It's an escalation ladder missing a rung. Frankopan's full Russia analysis drops next week.
items.tweetB: The Oreshnik ('hazelnut bush') was Russia's status symbol missile. Now it's being raised again in response to Moscow strikes. Either Russia is bluffing with its highest-profile system, or the calculus changed. Both scenarios are dangerous. Watch energy and defense positioning here.

items.title: Noblesse Oblige — wealthy visible disconnect from communities is driving anti-rich sentiment across the West
items.idea: Growing anti-wealthy sentiment in America and the West (less so in Asian countries so far) is driven by wealth inequality compounded by social media visibility of disconnect. 'Noblesse oblige' — the idea that power/wealth obligates you to your community — has largely vanished. American wealthy are actually among the most charitable globally, but this doesn't translate into perceived community embeddedness. Social media shows wealth without community context. The essay argues Asian countries haven't hit the same inflection yet — a potential lead indicator.
items.angle: This is a political risk frame. Anti-wealth backlash has clear downstream effects: wealth taxes, regulatory hostility, political targeting of specific sectors. The interesting structural point is that Asian countries haven't yet hit the same inflection — a lead indicator for what's coming.
items.tweetA: The wealthy in America are actually among the most charitable globally. Yet anti-rich sentiment has never been higher. The gap isn't generosity — it's visibility and embeddedness. Social media shows wealth without community context. Noblesse oblige worked when the lord lived in the village. There's no village on Instagram.
items.tweetB: Western anti-wealth backlash is politically real and accelerating. Asian countries haven't hit the same inflection yet — that's not a coincidence, it's a lead indicator. The question for anyone building or holding concentrated wealth: is the local community relationship there, or is it just the philanthropy announcement?

items.title: Japan's binding force is culture not religion — 62% want immigrants to follow customs; language fluency (not ethnicity) is the actual test
items.idea: Japan is rapidly accepting immigrants (3M citizens lost to deaths outpacing births in recent years) and grappling with identity questions that mirror America's. Key insight from cultural historian Hiroko Yoda: Japanese identity is anchored not in religion or ethnicity but in high-context culture — 'kuuki wo yomu' (reading the air). A Stanford survey found language fluency (not race) is the primary immigration acceptance criterion; 62% want immigrants to follow 'etiquette and customs.' Japan's 'yaoyorozu no kami' (eight million deities) = radically inclusive cosmology that absorbs rather than excludes. A far-right anti-immigrant party won surprising parliament seats in 2025 elections but demographics and cultural tradition are against them.
items.angle: This is a framework for thinking about national identity crises playing out across the developed world. The 'high-context culture as social glue' model is more robust than the religion-as-community model because it's portable — it can absorb outsiders through behavior, not belief. Has direct implications for immigration policy debates everywhere.
items.tweetA: Japan is aging fast (3M citizens lost in recent years), accepting more immigrants than ever, and asking: what does it mean to be Japanese? Answer from a Stanford survey: not ethnicity, not religion. Language fluency — which in Japanese means absorbing implicit cultural context, 'reading the air.' 62% want immigrants to follow customs, not just rules. Culture is the constitution.
items.tweetB: Americans assume faith is the spine of community. Japan runs the counter-experiment: 'born Shinto, married Christian, buried Buddhist' — they pick from all traditions and claim no faith. Yet social cohesion holds. The binding force is high-context culture, not doctrine. As the US debates its identity crisis, the Japanese model is worth more attention than it gets.
